```python
from .util import ErrorHandler
...

...
```

Early in the Treeherder days we made quite a few decision decisions that were in the name of performance, but ultimately made the architecture more complicated - and it turned out afterwards that the performance would have been just fine had we not done so. As such we're making a concerted effort moving forwards not to [prematurely optimise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Program_optimization#When_to_optimize).

For this case and similar below, I'd first do it the ORM way, then later once it's deployed and on New Relic we can see about query optimisation or adding Redis caching to certain calls.

It's important to `return ax` in any function that plots stuff.

Each of pyfolio's tear sheets should be one single image: that is, if I saved it the whole tear sheet should save as a single png, not one png for each plot in the tear sheet. This is to make it easier for the analysis to be saved, passed around, etc. We go to great lengths in `tears.py` to achieve this, and the way we do it is by making a single `matplotlib` figure with several axes, and pass these axes to functions in other modules: `tears.py` then expects the axes back, with the stuff having been plotted.

We also don't lose the "just plot" functionality with this method either, because if a user just calls `plot_foo` and _doesn't_ pass in an axes, then `ax=None`, `plt.gca()` and the `ax` that gets returned is just thrown away, leaving only the plotted output to be shown with `plt.show()`. So we don't really lose anything here.

This won't work with Py3 as there is no `file` constant there.

The proper way to check this in py3 is to do:
```python
>>> from io import IOBase
>>> isinstance(open('/tmp/demo2', 'w'), IOBase)
True
```

This however doesn't work in Py2 so we should probably do:
```
try:
    file_type = file  # Python 2
except NameError:
    file_type = IOBase  # Python 3

# ...
if isinstance(source_code, file_type):
```

The project's style for acceptance tests has evolved somewhat since many of these were written. This one reflects the current style, which adopts the perspective that the "user" in these user acceptance tests is a _developer_ using the _API_. So the scenario name is usually terse like this, often {object}.{method or property}, sometimes with "getter" or "setter" appended when it's two aspects of a property.

The given statement in this style mentions the "variable" name used for the object in an "as" clause at the end.

This test is quite simplified from your original version. This reflects that there are three "levels" or "steps" that are tested, each individually:

1. A document object has a `.bookmarks` property that returns (unconditionally and idempotently in this case) a `Bookmarks` object.

2. A Bookmarks object has certain behaviors (len, indexed access, interable, etc.)

3. An individual _Bookmark_ object has certain behaviors.

These are each tested separately and not mixed in one test.

These actions make sense, but you can't test `STDOUT` or deisctl actually trying to ssh into a machine. So I created `doJournal` which allows me to replace `STDOUT` and `ssh` with stubs that can actually be tested. I then had `Journal` call `doJournal` with `STDOUT` and `ssh`, keeping the public api the same.

Sphinx-gallery can execute the tutorial code for you while building the docs. I think that is a fantastic option and it would catch syntax and import errors in the docs for free.

There are 2 ways to do it:

1) We can add a regex to actually select and execute the tutorial code. 
2) We can change the file names to start with the prefix "plot_" and it will automatically execute the tutorials and catch syntax and import errors. For example "docs/gallery/general/file.py" --> "docs/gallery/general/plot_file.py". I think that is sphinx-gallery's convention.

See https://sphinx-gallery.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced_configuration.html#parsing-and-executing-examples-based-on-matching-patterns

In the future it would be great to assert some cases against tutorial code. I would be interested in doing that work but that is another PR.
